literal: oil, grease, viscidity, stickiness; from √snih 'to be sticky/moist, to be fond of' — figuratively the 'oiliness' of the heart: affection, fondness, tender love
invariant_definition: *Sneha* is affection or fondness — literally 'oiliness, viscidity' (√snih, 'to be sticky, to be moist with feeling'), the heart's tender adhesion to what is dear. In the *Bhagavad-Gītā* it appears at 2.57 in the negated compound *anabhisneha*: the man of settled wisdom is *sarvatra anabhisneha*, 'without fondness anywhere', meeting fortune and misfortune without elation or recoil. The commentators treat it as a movement of the mind: Madhusūdana Sarasvatī glosses the *sneha* to be given up as *premāpara-paryāyas tāmaso vṛtti-viśeṣaḥ* — 'a tāmasic particular modification of mind, another name for love (*prema*)' — by which another's loss or gain is superimposed upon oneself; he adds that *sneha* toward the Lord is to be kept, only fondness for the non-self being renounced. *Sneha* is close to *saṅga* (clinging) and *rāga* (the mind's coloring) but is not identical to either: where *saṅga* is the adhering of √sañj and *rāga* the dyeing of √rañj, *sneha* is the warm, oily tenderness of √snih — affection felt, not merely attachment formed.

per_school.definition: At 2.57 the *sthitaprajña* is *sarvatra anabhisneha* — 'without fondness anywhere' — neither rejoicing on gaining the auspicious nor recoiling from the inauspicious. Śaṅkara reads *anabhisneha* as the absence of attachment even toward body and life; Madhusūdana defines the renounced *sneha* as a *tāmasa vṛtti-viśeṣa*, a synonym of *prema*, and is careful to except *sneha* toward the Lord — only fondness for the non-self is to be removed.
primary_loci: Madhusūdana, Gūḍhārtha-dīpikā ad 2.57 (premāpara-paryāyas tāmaso vṛtti-viśeṣaḥ snehaḥ)

translator_note: Render *sneha* as **'affection'** — the warm, emotional love-bond of the heart, keeping its root sense of 'oiliness' (√snih). It is NOT *saṅga* (clinging/adhering, √sañj) nor *rāga* (the mind's coloring, √rañj): *sneha* is specifically affective. Madhusūdana fixes the sense precisely — it is *prema*-synonymous, the affection by which *another's loss or gain is superimposed upon oneself* (one feels a loved one's fortunes as one's own). So 'affection' is preferred over the weaker 'fondness' (reserve 'fondness' for the lighter *prīti*) and over 'attachment' (which is *saṅga*). The Gītā word at 2.57 is the negation *anabhisneha*, 'without affection [toward anything] anywhere' (*sarvatra*) — the sage has no such affective bond even toward body, life, or kin, so their gain brings no elation and their loss no grief.
